rule.name: Raw SQL via Raw() with String Concatenation
rule.rule_desc: Detects GORM Raw() calls with SQL built via string concatenation or fmt.Sprintf.
rule.vuln_desc: Building raw SQL strings with concatenation or Sprintf in GORM Raw() calls bypasses parameterization and introduces SQL injection, negating GORM's built-in protections.
rule.developer: Use GORM positional parameter bindings: db.Raw("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?", userID). Never concatenate or format user values into SQL strings.
rule.reviewer: Confirm all Raw() calls use positional ? or named @param bindings with no string concatenation of user input. This can be confirmed as an issue when the matched path is reachable and required controls are missing, weak, or inconsistent.

rule.name: Unparameterized Where() Clause
rule.rule_desc: Detects GORM Where() calls with conditions built via string concatenation or fmt.Sprintf.
rule.vuln_desc: Concatenating values into GORM Where() conditions bypasses prepared statement parameterization, enabling SQL injection via condition manipulation.
rule.developer: Use GORM's parameterized where syntax: db.Where("name = ?", name) or struct-based conditions: db.Where(&User{Name: name}).
rule.reviewer: Confirm Where() conditions use ? placeholders or struct-based filtering, not string formatting. This can be confirmed as an issue when the matched path is reachable and required controls are missing, weak, or inconsistent.

rule.name: Exec() with Unparameterized SQL
rule.rule_desc: Detects GORM Exec() calls executing dynamically built SQL strings.
rule.vuln_desc: Exec() with string-formatted SQL bypasses all ORM parameterization, enabling SQL injection in data manipulation operations.
rule.developer: Use Exec() with positional parameters: db.Exec("UPDATE users SET name = ? WHERE id = ?", name, id).
rule.reviewer: Verify all Exec() calls use parameterized SQL. This can be confirmed as an issue when the matched path is reachable and required controls are missing, weak, or inconsistent.
